name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
نَحْنُ خَلَقْنَـٰكُمْ فَلَوْلَا تُصَدِّقُونَ (٥٧)
```

```english
We created you. Then why do you not acknowledge it? (57)
```

```arabic
أَفَرَءَيْتُم مَّا تُمْنُونَ (٥٨)
```

```english
Have you considered the sperm that you emit? (58)
```

```arabic
ءَأَنتُمْ تَخْلُقُونَهُۥٓ أَمْ نَحْنُ ٱلْخَـٰلِقُونَ (٥٩)
```

```english
Is it you who create it, or are We the creator? (59)
```

```arabic
نَحْنُ قَدَّرْنَا بَيْنَكُمُ ٱلْمَوْتَ وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمَسْبُوقِينَ (٦٠)
```

```english
We have ordained death among you, and We are not to be outmaneuvered (60)
```

```arabic
عَلَىٰٓ أَن نُّبَدِّلَ أَمْثَـٰلَكُمْ وَنُنشِئَكُمْ فِى مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٦١)
```

```english
from replacing you with your likes and recreating you in [a realm] you do not know. (61)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ عَلِمْتُمُ ٱلنَّشْأَةَ ٱلْأُولَىٰ فَلَوْلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ (٦٢)
```

```english
Certainly you have known the first genesis, then why do you not take admonition? (62)
```

```arabic
أَفَرَءَيْتُم مَّا تَحْرُثُونَ (٦٣)
```

```english
Have you considered what you sow? (63)
```

```arabic
ءَأَنتُمْ تَزْرَعُونَهُۥٓ أَمْ نَحْنُ ٱلزَّٰرِعُونَ (٦٤)
```

```english
Is it you who make it grow, or are We the grower? (64)
```

```arabic
لَوْ نَشَآءُ لَجَعَلْنَـٰهُ حُطَـٰمًا فَظَلْتُمْ تَفَكَّهُونَ (٦٥)
```

```english
If We wish, We turn it into chaff, whereat you are left stunned [saying to yourselves,] (65)
```

```arabic
إِنَّا لَمُغْرَمُونَ (٦٦)
```

```english
‘Indeed we have suffered loss! (66)
```

```arabic
بَلْ نَحْنُ مَحْرُومُونَ (٦٧)
```

```english
No, we are deprived!’ (67)
```

```arabic
أَفَرَءَيْتُمُ ٱلْمَآءَ ٱلَّذِى تَشْرَبُونَ (٦٨)
```

```english
Have you considered the water that you drink? (68)
```

```arabic
ءَأَنتُمْ أَنزَلْتُمُوهُ مِنَ ٱلْمُزْنِ أَمْ نَحْنُ ٱلْمُنزِلُونَ (٦٩)
```

```english
Is it you who bring it down from the rain cloud, or is it We who bring [it] down? (69)
```

```arabic
لَوْ نَشَآءُ جَعَلْنَـٰهُ أُجَاجًا فَلَوْلَا تَشْكُرُونَ (٧٠)
```

```english
If We wish We can make it bitter. Then why do you not give thanks? (70)
```

```arabic
أَفَرَءَيْتُمُ ٱلنَّارَ ٱلَّتِى تُورُونَ (٧١)
```

```english
Have you considered the fire that you kindle? (71)
```

```arabic
ءَأَنتُمْ أَنشَأْتُمْ شَجَرَتَهَآ أَمْ نَحْنُ ٱلْمُنشِـُٔونَ (٧٢)
```

```english
Was it you who caused its tree to grow, or were We the grower? (72)
```

```arabic
نَحْنُ جَعَلْنَـٰهَا تَذْكِرَةً وَمَتَـٰعًا لِّلْمُقْوِينَ (٧٣)
```

```english
It was We who made it a reminder and a boon for the desert-dwellers. (73)
```

```arabic
فَسَبِّحْ بِٱسْمِ رَبِّكَ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٧٤)
```

```english
So celebrate the Name of your Lord, the All-supreme. (74)
```

```arabic
۞ فَلَآ أُقْسِمُ بِمَوَٰقِعِ ٱلنُّجُومِ (٧٥)
```

```english
I swear by the places where the stars set! (75)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّهُۥ لَقَسَمٌ لَّوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عَظِيمٌ (٧٦)
```

```english
And indeed it is a great oath, should you know. (76)
```

```arabic
إِنَّهُۥ لَقُرْءَانٌ كَرِيمٌ (٧٧)
```

```english
This is indeed a noble Quran, (77)
```

```arabic
فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مَّكْنُونٍ (٧٨)
```

```english
in a guarded Book (78)
```

```arabic
لَّا يَمَسُّهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱلْمُطَهَّرُونَ (٧٩)
```

```english
—no one touches it except the pure ones— (79)
```

```arabic
تَنزِيلٌ مِّن رَّبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٨٠)
```

```english
sent down gradually from the Lord of all the worlds. (80)
```

```arabic
أَفَبِهَـٰذَا ٱلْحَدِيثِ أَنتُم مُّدْهِنُونَ (٨١)
```

```english
What! Do you take lightly this discourse? (81)
```

```arabic
وَتَجْعَلُونَ رِزْقَكُمْ أَنَّكُمْ تُكَذِّبُونَ (٨٢)
```

```english
And make your denial of it your vocation? (82)
```

```arabic
فَلَوْلَآ إِذَا بَلَغَتِ ٱلْحُلْقُومَ (٨٣)
```

```english
So when it reaches the throat [of the dying person], (83)
```

```arabic
وَأَنتُمْ حِينَئِذٍ تَنظُرُونَ (٨٤)
```

```english
and at that moment you are looking on [at his bedside] (84)
```

```arabic
وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنكُمْ وَلَـٰكِن لَّا تُبْصِرُونَ (٨٥)
```

```english
—and We are nearer to him than you are, though you do not perceive— (85)
```

```arabic
فَلَوْلَآ إِن كُنتُمْ غَيْرَ مَدِينِينَ (٨٦)
```

```english
then why do you not restore it, if you are not subject (86)
```

```arabic
تَرْجِعُونَهَآ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٨٧)
```

```english
[to Divine dispensation], if you are truthful? (87)
```

```arabic
فَأَمَّآ إِن كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُقَرَّبِينَ (٨٨)
```

```english
Then, if he be of those brought near, (88)
```

```arabic
فَرَوْحٌ وَرَيْحَانٌ وَجَنَّتُ نَعِيمٍ (٨٩)
```

```english
then ease, abundance, and a garden of bliss. (89)
```

```arabic
وَأَمَّآ إِن كَانَ مِنْ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلْيَمِينِ (٩٠)
```

```english
And if he be of the People of the Right Hand, (90)
```

```arabic
فَسَلَـٰمٌ لَّكَ مِنْ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلْيَمِينِ (٩١)
```

```english
then [he will be told,] ‘Peace be on you, from the People of the Right Hand!’ (91)
```

```arabic
وَأَمَّآ إِن كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ ٱلضَّآلِّينَ (٩٢)
```

```english
But if he be of the impugners, the astray ones, (92)
```

```arabic
فَنُزُلٌ مِّنْ حَمِيمٍ (٩٣)
```

```english
then a treat of boiling water (93)
```

```arabic
وَتَصْلِيَةُ جَحِيمٍ (٩٤)
```

```english
and entry into hell. (94)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَهُوَ حَقُّ ٱلْيَقِينِ (٩٥)
```

```english
Indeed this is certain truth. (95)
```

```arabic
فَسَبِّحْ بِٱسْمِ رَبِّكَ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٩٦)
```

```english
So celebrate the Name of your Lord, the All-supreme! (96)
```

## (Commentary)

After the Glorified has detailed the final state of each of the three groups, He elaborated on the state of the "companions of the left" (أصحاب الشمال) and explained that what led them to that end was their breaking of the covenant of servitude (العبودية) and their denial of the Resurrection (البعث) and Retribution (الجزاء). He then commanded His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to respond to them by affirming the Resurrection and Retribution and clarifying what they will be recompensed with on the Day of Resurrection.

He rebuked them for their denial of the Return (المعاد) — *the restoration of life after death for judgment* — despite the fact that the One informing them of it is their Creator, who manages their affairs and ordains for them death and then the second creation (الإنشاء). Thus, He knows what befalls them throughout their existence and what their final state will be. Furthermore, the Book that informs them of the Return is a Noble Quran (قرآن كريم), protected from being tampered with by the hands of the devils and their misleading allies.

Then, the discourse returns to what it began with regarding the state of the three groups, mentioning that the divergence in the conditions of these categories begins from the moment of death, and with this, the Surah concludes.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **نَحْنُ خَلَقْناكُمْ فَلَوْ لا تُصَدِّقُونَ** — **"We created you. Then why do you not accept the truth?"** (56:57). The context is the discourse regarding the Resurrection and Retribution, which they had denied and rejected. Thus, His saying, **فَلَوْ لا تُصَدِّقُونَ** ("Then why do you not accept the truth?"), is an exhortation to believe in the account of the Return and to cease denying it. He justified this with His saying, **نَحْنُ خَلَقْناكُمْ** ("We created you"), as inferred from the causal derivation in His saying, **فَلَوْ لا تُصَدِّقُونَ**.

The fact that His creation of them necessitates the obligation of believing Him regarding what He informs of the Return is established from two aspects: First, that He, the Exalted, created them the first time, so He is capable of recreating them a second time, as He said: **قالَ مَنْ يُحْيِ الْعِظامَ وَهِيَ رَمِيمٌ قُلْ يُحْيِيهَا الَّذِي أَنْشَأَها أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ خَلْقٍ عَلِيمٌ** — **"He says, ‘Who will revive the bones when they have decayed?’ Say, ‘He will revive them who produced them the first time, and He has knowledge of all creation.’"** (36:78-79).

Second, since He, the Exalted, is their Creator and the Manager of their affairs, who ordains the specifics of their creation and their command, He is most knowledgeable of what He will do with them and what will befall them. Therefore, when He informs them that He will resurrect them after their death and recompense them for what they have done, whether good or evil, there is no choice but to believe Him. There is no excuse for one who denies what His Book has informed regarding the Resurrection and Retribution. The Exalted said: **أَلا يَعْلَمُ مَنْ خَلَقَ وَهُوَ اللَّطِيفُ الْخَبِيرُ** — **"Does He who created not know? And He is the All-attentive, the All-aware."** (67:14). And He said: **كَما بَدَأْنا أَوَّلَ خَلْقٍ نُعِيدُهُ وَعْداً عَلَيْنا إِنَّا كُنَّا فاعِلِينَ** — **"As We originated the first creation, We will bring it back—a promise [binding] on Us; We will indeed do it."** (21:104). And He said: **وَعْدَ اللهِ حَقًّا وَمَنْ أَصْدَقُ مِنَ اللهِ قِيلاً** — **"Allah’s promise is true, and who is more truthful in speech than Allah?"** (4:122).

The summary of the verse is: We created you and We know what We have done and what We shall do with you; thus, We inform you that We shall resurrect you and recompense you for what you have done—so why do you not believe what We inform you of in the Book We have sent down?

In this verse and those following it, there is a transition (التفات) from the third person to the second person (address), because the context is one of rebuke and censure, and that is more impactful and emphatic through direct address.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَفَرَأَيْتُمْ ما تُمْنُونَ** — **"Have you considered the sperm that you emit?"** (56:58). Emission (الأمناء) is the ejection and pouring of semen, and what is meant is its ejection into the wombs. The meaning is: Have you considered the semen that you pour into the wombs of women?

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَأَنْتُمْ تَخْلُقُونَهُ أَمْ نَحْنُ الْخالِقُونَ** — **"Is it you who create it, or are We the creators?"** (56:59). That is, do you create it into a human being like yourselves, or are We the creators of it as a human being?

**His saying, the Exalted:** **نَحْنُ قَدَّرْنا بَيْنَكُمُ الْمَوْتَ وَما نَحْنُ بِمَسْبُوقِينَ** — **"We have ordained death among you, and We are not to be outrun,"** (56:60). The management of the affairs of creation in all its aspects and specifics is a necessary concomitant of creation in the sense of the emanation of existence (إفاضة الوجود). Thus, the existence of a human being, bounded from the beginning of his formation to the last moment of his worldly life, with all the specifics that transform within him, is by the ordainment (تقدير) of his Creator, Mighty and Majestic. Therefore, his death, like his life, is by an ordainment from Him. Death does not befall him due to a deficiency in the power of his Creator to create him such that death does not befall him, nor due to causes and factors that affect him with death and thereby nullify the life that his Creator, the Exalted, emanated upon him. For the necessary consequence of that would be that His power, the Exalted, is limited and deficient, and that some causes could frustrate Him and their will could overcome His will—which is impossible. How could it be so, when Power is absolute and the Will is never overcome?

It becomes clear from this that the meaning of His saying, **نَحْنُ قَدَّرْنا بَيْنَكُمُ الْمَوْتَ** ("We have ordained death among you"), is that death is a decreed truth and not something necessitated or required by the mode of existence of the living being; rather, He, the Exalted, ordained for him such an existence and then a death to follow it.

And the meaning of His saying, **وَما نَحْنُ بِمَسْبُوقِينَ** ("and We are not to be outrun"), where outrunning (السبق) means overcoming and the one outrun (المسبوق) is the one overcome—is: We are not overcome in the occurrence of death by the causes associated with it, such that We emanation life upon you intending for it to endure, but then the causes outrun Us and overcome Us, nullifying the life whose endurance We had intended.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **عَلى أَنْ نُبَدِّلَ أَمْثالَكُمْ وَنُنْشِئَكُمْ فِي ما لا تَعْلَمُونَ** — **"that We may replace you with your likes and produce you in [a state] you do not know."** (56:61). The preposition **عَلى** ("on/upon") is connected to His saying, **قَدَّرْنا** ("We ordained"), and the prepositional phrase is in the position of a circumstantial clause (حال); that is: We ordained death among you while it is based upon the replacement of likes and the production in that which you do not know.

Likes (الأمثال) is the plural of *mithl* (مثل), which refers to that which is identical to a thing in its kind, like one human individual in relation to another. The meaning of His saying, **أَنْ نُبَدِّلَ أَمْثالَكُمْ** ("that We may replace your likes"), is that We replace your likes from among humanity with you, or We replace your likes in your stead. The meaning in any case is the replacement of one group with another and the placing of successors in the stead of predecessors.

His saying, **وَنُنْشِئَكُمْ فِي ما لا تَعْلَمُونَ** ("and produce you in [a state] you do not know"), where **ما** is a relative pronoun referring to the creation, and the sentence is coordinated with **نُبَدِّلَ** ("replace"). The implied meaning is: and upon [the basis] that We produce you and bring you into existence in another creation that you do not know, which is the otherworldly existence (الوجود الأخروي) as opposed to the transient worldly existence.

The summary of the meaning of the two verses is that death among you is only by an ordainment from Us, not due to a deficiency in Our power such that the continuation of your life is not feasible for Us, nor due to the dominance of destructive and annihilating causes that frustrate Us in preserving your life. Rather, We have ordained it among you on the basis of replacing likes—removing a people and bringing others—and producing a creation for you that suits the afterlife, beyond the perishing worldly creation. Thus, death is a transition from one abode to another and a transformation of creation into another creation, not an annihilation or non-existence.

Some have suggested that "likes" (الأمثال) in the verse is the plural of *mathal* (مثل) with two *fathas*, meaning "description" or "attribute" (الوصف). Thus, the two phrases **عَلى أَنْ نُبَدِّلَ** ("that We may replace") etc., and **وَنُنْشِئَكُمْ** ("and produce you") etc., would convey a single meaning. The sense would be: that We change your attributes and produce you in an attribute you do not recognize or know, such as your being gathered in the form of a dog or a pig or other animals after you were in the world in the form of a human. However, the previous meaning is more comprehensive and more beneficial.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَقَدْ عَلِمْتُمُ النَّشْأَةَ الْأُولى فَلَوْ لا تَذَكَّرُونَ** — **"Certainly you have known the first genesis, then why do you not take admonition?"** (56:62). What is meant by the first genesis (النشأة الأولى) is the worldly genesis. Knowledge of it with its specifics necessitates the admission of another eternal genesis in which there is retribution. For it is known from the cosmic order that there is no futility or falsehood in existence; therefore, this transient genesis must have an enduring end. Furthermore, it is among the necessities of this order that everything be guided to the happiness of its kind, and the guidance of man requires the sending of messengers, the legislating of laws, and the directing of commands and prohibitions, and the retribution for good and evil deeds—which does not occur in this world, so it must be in another abode, which is the next genesis (النشأة الآخرة).

Moreover, they witnessed the first genesis and knew it, and they knew that the One who brought it into existence from the depths of non-existence is Allah, the Glorified. Since He was capable of it the first time, He is capable of bringing its like into existence a second time. The Exalted said: **قُلْ يُحْيِيهَا الَّذِي أَنْشَأَها أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ** — **"Say, ‘He will revive them who produced them the first time’"** (36:79). This is a proof of possibility (الإمكان) by which their deeming the Resurrection improbable is removed.

In short, through their knowledge of the first genesis, they attain knowledge of the premises of the proof for the possibility of the Resurrection, thereby removing the sense of improbability; for there is no improbability where there is possibility.

This—as you see—is a proof for the possibility of the resurrection of bodies (حشر الأجساد). Its summary is that the resurrected body is the like of the worldly body, and since the making and reviving of the worldly body was permissible, the making and reviving of the otherworldly body must also be permissible, because it is its like, and the ruling for likes (الأمثال) regarding what is permissible and what is not is one and the same.

Thus, it is surprising what al-Zamakhshari said in *al-Kashshaf* regarding the verse: "In this is a proof for the validity of analogy (القياس), as He charged them with ignorance for failing to analogize the next genesis to the first." (End of quote). This is because what is in the verse is a logical demonstrative syllogism (قياس برهاني منطقي), whereas what he seeks to prove by it is a jurisprudential analogy (قياس فقهي) which yields only conjecture (الظن)—so how far apart are the two?

And it is said in *Ruh al-Ma'ani* regarding the verse: "Then why do you not remember that He who was capable of it—meaning the first genesis—is even more capable of the next genesis? For it involves less 'making' due to the availability of materials, the specification of parts, and the precedence of the model (المثال). This, according to what they said, is a proof for the validity of analogy, though it was said: it only indicates the 'analogy of the a fortiori' (قياس الأولي) because that is what is in the verse." (End of quote).

In this is the same error as the previous one. Furthermore, what is in the verse is not an "analogy of the a fortiori" at all, because the common link between the first and the next genesis is that they are two likes, and the principle of the syllogism is that the ruling for likes regarding what is permissible and what is not is one.

As for his saying that the next genesis involves "less making" due to the availability of materials and the specification of parts, this is rejected. For materials require the emanation of existence for their subsistence (بقاء) just as they require it for their origination (حدوث) and their first attainment. Likewise, the specification of parts requires it for subsistence just as it requires it initially; thus, the making the second time is like the making the first time.

As for his saying, "and the precedence of the model (المثال)," he has confused the "like" (المثل) with the "model" (المثال). The otherworldly body, in relation to itself, is the *like* of the worldly body, not according to its *model*; for if it were according to its model, the Hereafter would be the world and not the Hereafter.

**If you say:** If the otherworldly body is a *like* (مثل) of the worldly body, and the like of a thing is other than it, then the human being returned in the Hereafter would be other than the human being started in the world, because he is his like, not his very self (عينه).

**I say:** It has been mentioned in previous discussions more than once that the identity (شخصية) of a human being is by his spirit (الروح), not by his body. The spirit is not annihilated by death; rather, the body decays and its parts scatter. Then, when it is fashioned a second time like what it was in the world and the spirit is attached to it, the human being is the very same human being who was in the world, just as the elderly Zayd is the very same as the young Zayd, due to the persistence of the spirit in its identity despite the body changing moment after moment.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَفَرَأَيْتُمْ ما تَحْرُثُونَ** — **"Have you considered what you sow?"** (56:63) until His saying **مَحْرُومُونَ** — **"deprived."** (56:67). After He reminded them of the manner of their own creation and the ordainment of death among them as a preparation for the Resurrection and Retribution—all of which are concomitants of His Lordship (ربوبيته)—He enumerated for them three matters among the most important things by which they live in the world: the crop (الزرع) which they eat, the water (الماء) which they drink, and the fire (النار) by which they warm themselves and which they use for many of their needs. Through these, His Lordship over them is established, for Lordship is nothing but management (التدبير) arising from ownership.

So He said: **أَفَرَأَيْتُمْ ما تَحْرُثُونَ** ("Have you considered what you sow?"). Sowing (الحرث) is working the land and casting the seed upon it. **أَأَنْتُمْ تَزْرَعُونَهُ** ("Is it you who make it grow?")—that is, do you cause it to sprout and develop until it reaches its end? The pronoun in **تَزْرَعُونَهُ** refers to the seed or the sowing known from the context. **أَمْ نَحْنُ الزَّارِعُونَ** ("or are We the growers?")—the ones who cause it to sprout and develop until it is complete as a crop. **لَوْ نَشاءُ لَجَعَلْناهُ حُطاماً** ("If We wished, We would surely turn it into chaff")—that is, broken, crumbled, and dry. **فَظَلْتُمْ** ("and you would be left")—that is, you would become **تَفَكَّهُونَ** ("wondering")—that is, marveling at what has afflicted your crop and talking about what happened, saying: **إِنَّا لَمُغْرَمُونَ** ("‘We are indeed debt-ridden!’")—fallen into debt and loss; our wealth is gone, our time wasted, and our effort failed. **بَلْ نَحْنُ مَحْرُومُونَ** ("‘Indeed we are deprived!’")—prevented from provision and good.

There is no contradiction between denying the "growing" (الزرع) from them and attributing it to Him, the Exalted, and the mediation of natural factors and causes in the sprouting and growth of the crop. For the discussion returns to the influence and making of these causes; their mode of influence is not by an exigency of their own essence independent of Him, the Exalted, but rather by His making, placing, and gifting. The same applies to the causes of these causes, and the matter ends with Allah, the Glorified—and indeed, to your Lord is the final end.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَفَرَأَيْتُمُ الْماءَ الَّذِي تَشْرَبُونَ** — **"Have you considered the water that you drink?"** (56:68) until His saying **فَلَوْ لا تَشْكُرُونَ** — **"Then why do you not give thanks?"** (56:70). The clouds (المزن) are the rain-clouds. His saying, **فَلَوْ لا تَشْكُرُونَ** ("Then why do you not give thanks?"), is an exhortation to gratitude (الشكر). Gratitude to Him, the Exalted, is the beautiful mention of Him for His blessings, which is the manifestation of one's servitude (العبادة) in word and deed. The rest is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَفَرَأَيْتُمُ النَّارَ الَّتِي تُورُون** — **"Have you considered the fire that you strike?"** (56:71) until His saying **وَمَتاعاً لِلْمُقْوِينَ** — **"and a convenience for the desert-travelers."** (56:73). It is said in *al-Majma'*: Striking (الإيراء) is the manifestation of fire by friction; it is said *awra yuri*. He said: It is said "he struck and it ignited" (أورى) when it appears, and if it does not ignite, it is said "he struck and it failed" (أكبى). He said: The traveler (المقوي) is the one who alights in the desert (القواء) where there is no one; and a house is said to be *aqwat* when it is empty of its inhabitants. (End of quote). The meaning is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَسَبِّحْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ الْعَظِيمِ** — **"So celebrate the Name of your Lord, the All-supreme."** (56:74). This is an address to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). When He, the Glorified, mentioned the evidences of His Lordship over them—that He is the one who creates them and manages their affairs, and that part of His management is that He will resurrect them and recompense them for their deeds while they are deniers of that—He turned away from addressing them and turned to addressing the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), indicating that they do not comprehend the speech. Thus, He commanded the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to hallow Him, the Exalted, from their associating partners with Him and their denial of the Resurrection and Retribution.

So His saying, **فَسَبِّحْ بِاسْمِ** ("So celebrate the Name") etc., the *fa* is for the derivation of the glorification (التسبيح) from the preceding explanation. The *ba* is for seeking assistance (الاستعانة) or accompaniment (الملابسة). The meaning is: Since it is so, then glorify while seeking assistance through the mention of the Name of your Lord. Or what is meant by the Name (الاسم) is the mention (الذكر), because the utterance of the name of a thing is a mention of it, as has been said. Or the *ba* is for transitivity (التعدية), because the hallowing of the name of a thing is the hallowing of the thing itself. The meaning is: Hallow the Name of your Lord from mentioning a partner for Him or denying the Resurrection and Retribution from Him. "The All-supreme" (العظيم) is an attribute of the Lord or the Name.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَلا أُقْسِمُ بِمَواقِعِ النُّجُومِ** — **"I swear by the places where the stars set!"** (56:75). **فَلا أُقْسِمُ** is an oath. It is said that *la* is extra and *uqsimu* is the oath. It is also said that *la* is negating and *uqsimu* is the oath.

"Places" (مواقع) is the plural of *mawqi'* (موقع), which is the location. The meaning is: I swear by the locations of the stars in the sky. It is also said that *mawaqi'* is the plural of *mawqi'*, a mim-infinitive meaning "falling," referring to the falling of the stars on the Day of Resurrection, or the falling of meteors upon the devils, or the setting-places of the stars in the west. The first of these views is the one that first comes to mind.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنَّهُ لَقَسَمٌ لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عَظِيمٌ** — **"And it is indeed a great oath, if you only knew!"** (56:76). This is an exaltation of this oath and an emphasis upon emphasis.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّهُ لَقُرْآنٌ كَرِيمٌ** — **"This is indeed a noble Quran,"** (56:77) until His saying **مِنْ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ** — **"from the Lord of all the worlds."** (56:80). Since their denial of the account of His Oneness (وحدانيته) in Lordship and Divinity, as well as their denial of the Resurrection and Retribution, was manifested through their denial of the Quran revealed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—which contains the news of Monotheism (التوحيد) and the Resurrection—their denial branched into two: a denial of the origin of Monotheism and the Resurrection fundamentally, and a denial of that insofar as the Quran informs them of it. Therefore, He, the Exalted, first presented an explanation to establish the origin of Oneness and the Resurrection by mentioning evidences from His signs that prove that, which is His saying: **نَحْنُ خَلَقْناكُمْ** ("We created you") until His saying **وَمَتاعاً لِلْمُقْوِينَ** ("and a convenience for the desert-travelers"). Secondly, He presented an explanation emphasizing that the Noble Quran is His speech, preserved with Him and revealed from Him, describing it with its finest attributes.

So His saying, **إِنَّهُ لَقُرْآنٌ كَرِيمٌ** ("This is indeed a noble Quran"), is the response to the preceding oath. The pronoun refers to the Quran known from the preceding context. It is inferred from its description as "noble" (كريم) without restriction in a context of praise that it is noble to Allah and dear to Him, noble in its praised attributes, and noble in its generosity and benefit to people due to the principles of knowledge (المعارف) it contains, which lead to the happiness of this world and the Hereafter.

His saying, **فِي كِتابٍ مَكْنُونٍ** — **"in a protected Book,"** (56:78) is a second attribute of the Quran; that is, preserved and protected from change and substitution. It is the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ), as He, the Exalted, said: **بَلْ هُوَ قُرْآنٌ مَجِيدٌ فِي لَوْحٍ مَحْفُوظٍ** — **"Indeed it is a glorious Quran, in a preserved tablet."** (85:21-22).

His saying, **لا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ** — **"no one touches it except the pure ones."** (56:79). This is an attribute of the "protected Book," and it is possible for it to be a third attribute of the Quran. The outcome of both views, assuming *la* is negating, is the same.

The meaning is: No one touches the protected Book in which the Quran is contained except the pure ones, or no one touches the Quran which is in the Book except the pure ones.

The speech, in any case, is intended to exalt the matter of the Quran and glorify it. Thus, "touching" (المس) it is the knowledge (العلم) of it while it is in the protected Book, as indicated by His saying: **إِنَّا جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتابِ لَدَيْنا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ** — **"We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. And it is indeed with Us in the Mother Book, [and it is] surely high and wise."** (43:3-4).

The "pure ones" (المطهرون)—the passive participle of "purification" (التطهير)—are those whose souls Allah, the Exalted, has purified from the filth of disobedience and the impurities of sins, or from that which is even greater and more subtle: the purification of their hearts from attachment to other than Him, the Exalted. This meaning of purification is what is appropriate for "touching," which is knowledge, rather than purification from physical impurity (الخبث) or ritual impurity (الحدث), as is apparent.

Thus, the pure ones are those whom Allah, the Exalted, has honored by purifying their souls, such as the noble angels and those among humans whom Allah has purified. The Exalted said: **إِنَّما يُرِيدُ اللهُ لِيُذْهِبَ عَنْكُمُ الرِّجْسَ أَهْلَ الْبَيْتِ وَيُطَهِّرَكُمْ تَطْهِيراً** — **"Indeed Allah desires to repel all impurity from you, O People of the Household, and purify you with a thorough purification."** (33:33). There is no reason to restrict the "pure ones" to the angels, as most commentators have done, for that would be a restriction without a restrictor.

It is sometimes suggested that *la* in **لا يَمَسُّهُ** is prohibitive (ناهية), and "touching" in this case refers to touching the script of the Quran, and "purity" refers to purity from ritual impurity, or both ritual and physical impurity. It has also been recited as *al-muttahhirun* (المطهرون) with a shadda on the *ta* and *ha* and a kasra on the *ha*, meaning "those who purify themselves." The implication of the verse would then be the prohibition of touching the script of the Quran without purity.

It is possible to carry the verse to this meaning even if *la* is negating, by the sentence being an informative statement intended as a command (إنشاء), which is more eloquent than a direct command.

Al-Zamakhshari said in *al-Kashshaf*: "If you make it—meaning the sentence **لا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ**—an attribute of the Quran, the meaning is: It is not fitting that anyone should touch it except one who is in a state of purity among people, meaning touching the written form of it." (End of quote). You have already seen the validity of intending "touching" as knowledge and awareness, assuming it is an attribute of the Quran, just as it is valid assuming it is an attribute of the protected Book.

His saying, **تَنْزِيلٌ مِنْ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ** — **"sent down from the Lord of all the worlds."** (56:80). This is another attribute of the Quran. The infinitive is in the sense of the passive participle; that is, "sent down" (منزل) from Allah to you, so that you may understand and reason it after it was in a protected Book that none touch except the pure ones.

The expression of Him, the Exalted, as "the Lord of all the worlds" (رب العالمين) is to indicate that His Lordship, the Exalted, is spread over all the worlds, and they are among them. Thus, He, the Exalted, is their Lord, and if He is their Lord, it is incumbent upon them to believe in His Book, listen to His speech, and accept it as true without denial.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَفَبِهذَا الْحَدِيثِ أَنْتُمْ مُدْهِنُونَ** — **"Do you then take this discourse lightly,"** (56:81). The reference in "this discourse" (بهذا الحديث) is to the Quran. "Taking lightly" (الإدهان) is to be negligent or compromising regarding it. Its origin is "softening with oil" (التليين بالدهن), used metaphorically for negligence. The interrogative is for rebuke; He, the Exalted, rebukes them for considering the matter of the Quran as something trivial not to be cared for.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَتَجْعَلُونَ رِزْقَكُمْ أَنَّكُمْ تُكَذِّبُونَ** — **"and make your denial your [only] share [of it]?"** (56:82). It is said that "provision" (الرزق) refers to their share of good. The meaning is: You make your share of the good that you could attain through the Quran to be your denial of it; that is, you put denial in its place. It is also said that "provision" refers to the Quran itself, which Allah provided for them. The meaning is: You take denial in place of this provision that you were given. It is also said that the speech has a suppressed mudaf (possessive), and the implied meaning is: "And you make the *gratitude* for your provision to be your denial"—that is, you have put denial in the place of gratitude.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَلَوْ لا إِذا بَلَغَتِ الْحُلْقُومَ** — **"So when it reaches the throat,"** (56:83) until His saying **صادِقِينَ** — **"truthful."** (56:87). This is a return to the beginning of the discourse by deriving from their denial that: If you are truthful in your negation of the Resurrection and correct in your denial of this Quran which informs you of the Resurrection, then return the soul of the dying person when it has reached the throat. For if death were not by an ordainment from Allah, it would be among the accidental matters for which a stratagem might be found to repel it. Since you are unable to return it and restore life with it, then know that death is a truth ordained by Allah to drive the souls toward the Resurrection and Retribution.

So His saying, **فَلَوْ لا إِذا بَلَغَتِ الْحُلْقُومَ**, is derived from their denial of the Quran and what it informs of the Resurrection and Retribution. **لو لا** is for exhortation (التحضيض) to demonstrate their incapacity and to silence them. The pronoun in **بَلَغَتِ** ("it reaches") refers to the soul (النفس), and the soul reaching the throat is a metaphor for the complete imminence of death.

His saying, **وَأَنْتُمْ حِينَئِذٍ تَنْظُرُونَ** ("and you are looking on at that moment")—that is, you are looking at the dying person; he is within your sight.

His saying, **وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْكُمْ وَلكِنْ لا تُبْصِرُونَ** ("and We are nearer to him than you are, but you do not perceive")—that is, while the case is that We are nearer to him than you are due to Our encompassing him in existence, and Our messengers who seize his soul are nearer to him than you are, but you do not see Us nor Our messengers.

The Exalted said: **اللهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنْفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِها** — **"Allah takes the souls at the time of their death"** (39:42). And He said: **قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ** — **"Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take you’"** (32:11). And He said: **حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ تَوفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنا** — **"until when death comes to one of you, Our messengers take him"** (6:61).

His saying, **فَلَوْ لا إِنْ كُنْتُمْ غَيْرَ مَدِينِينَ** ("then why do you not—if you are not to be judged")—the repetition of *law la* is to emphasize the previous *law la*. "Judged" (مدينين) means recompensed, from *dana yadinu* meaning *jaza yajzi*. The meaning is: If you are not to be recompensed with reward and punishment through the Resurrection.

His saying, **تَرْجِعُونَها إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ** ("restore it, if you are truthful?")—that is, if you are truthful in your claim that there is no Resurrection and no Retribution. His saying **تَرْجِعُونَها** ("restore it") is the object of the exhortative *law la* according to the implied structure. The arrangement of the verses according to the implied structure is: "Then why do you not restore it when it reaches the throat, if you are not to be judged?"

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَأَمَّا إِنْ كانَ مِنَ الْمُقَرَّبِينَ فَرَوْحٌ وَرَيْحانٌ وَجَنَّةُ نَعِيمٍ** — **"Then if he be of those brought near, then [there shall be] ease, abundance, and a garden of bliss."** (56:88-89). This is a return to the explanation of the state of the three groups mentioned at the beginning of the Surah at the time of death and after it. The pronoun in **كانَ** ("he be") refers to the deceased, known from the context. What is meant by "those brought near" (المقربين) are the "foremost, those brought near" (السابقون المقربون) mentioned previously. "Ease" (الروح) is comfort. "Abundance" (الريحان) is provision (الرزق). It is also said that it is the fragrant basil (الريحان) from the basil of Paradise brought to him, which he smells and then passes away.

The meaning is: As for if the deceased is among those brought near, then for him—or his recompense is—comfort from every worry, grief, and pain, and provision from the provision of Paradise, and a garden of bliss.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَمَّا إِنْ كانَ مِنْ أَصْحابِ الْيَمِينِ فَسَلامٌ لَكَ مِنْ أَصْحابِ الْيَمِينِ** — **"And if he be of the companions of the right, then ‘Peace be to you, from the companions of the right.’"** (56:90-91). It is possible that the *lam* is for possessive specialization (الاختصاص الملكي), and the meaning of **فَسَلامٌ لَكَ** is that you are specialized with peace from the companions of the right who are your peers and companions; thus, you see nothing from them but good and peace.

It is also said that *laka* (to you) means *'alayka* (upon you); that is, the companions of the right greet you with peace. Other things have also been said.

The transition from the third person to the second person (address) is to indicate that he is addressed with this greeting: "Peace be to you from the companions of the right."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَمَّا إِنْ كانَ مِنَ الْمُكَذِّبِينَ الضَّالِّينَ فَنُزُلٌ مِنْ حَمِيمٍ وَتَصْلِيَةُ جَحِيمٍ** — **"But if he be of the deniers, the astray, then a hospitality of boiling water, and entry into hell."** (56:92-94). "Entry" (تصلية) into the Fire is being made to enter it. It is also said: enduring its heat and punishment.

The meaning is: But if he be of the people of denial and straying, then for them is a hospitality of water of intense heat, and the enduring of the heat of the fire of Hell.

Allah has described them as "the deniers, the astray," placing denial before straying because the punishment they encounter is a consequence of their denial and their stubbornness against the truth. If it were straying without denial and stubbornness, they would be among the "oppressed" (المستضعفين) and would not occupy this station. As for His previous saying: **ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ أَيُّهَا الضَّالُّونَ الْمُكَذِّبُونَ** ("Then indeed, you, O astray deniers"), since the context there was one of responding to their saying: **أَإِذا مِتْنا وَكُنَّا تُراباً وَعِظاماً أَإِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ** ("When we are dead and have become dust and bones, shall we be resurrected?"), it was more appropriate to describe them first with straying and then with denial.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ هذا لَهُوَ حَقُّ الْيَقِينِ** — **"Indeed this is the certain truth."** (56:95). "Truth" (الحق) is knowledge insofar as the external reality corresponds to it. "Certainty" (اليقين) is knowledge in which there is no confusion or doubt. Thus, the annexation of "truth" to "certainty" is a type of explanatory annexation (إضافة بيانية) brought for emphasis.

The meaning is: Indeed, this which We have mentioned regarding the state of the three groups of people is the truth in which there is no hesitation, and the knowledge which no doubt affects.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَسَبِّحْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ الْعَظِيمِ** — **"So celebrate the Name of your Lord, the All-supreme."** (56:96). Its interpretation has preceded. It is derived from what preceded it of the description of the Quran and the explanation of the state of the three groups after death and in the Gathering.

The meaning is: Since the Quran is of these attributes and is truthful in what it informs of the state of people after death, then hallow your All-supreme Lord while seeking assistance from or being accompanied by His Name, and negate what these astray deniers see and claim.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma'*: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: **أَأَنْتُمْ تَزْرَعُونَهُ أَمْ نَحْنُ الزَّارِعُونَ** ("Is it you who make it grow, or are We the growers?"). It is narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that he said: **لا يقولن أحدكم زرعت وليقل حرثت** — "None of you should say, 'I grew [the crop],' but let him say, 'I sowed [the crop].'"

**I say:** It is also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* by several compilers of collections from Abu Hurayrah from him (ص).

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: **أَأَنْتُمْ أَنْزَلْتُمُوهُ مِنَ الْمُزْنِ** ("Is it you who sent it down from the rain-clouds?"). He said: "From the clouds." **نَحْنُ جَعَلْناها تَذْكِرَةً** ("We have made it a reminder")—for the fire of the Day of Resurrection. **وَمَتاعاً لِلْمُقْوِينَ** ("and a convenience for the desert-travelers"). He said: "The needy."

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **فَسَبِّحْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ الْعَظِيمِ** ("So celebrate the Name of your Lord, the All-supreme"). It is authentically reported from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that when this verse was revealed, he said: **اجعلوها في ركوعكم** — "Place it in your bowing (ركوع)."

**I say:** It is narrated in *al-Faqih* as a *mursal* report, and narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from al-Juhani from him (ص).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: al-Nasa'i, Ibn Jarir, Muhammad ibn Nasr, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in *Shu'ab al-Iman* narrated from Ibn Abbas that he said: "The Quran was sent down on the Night of Ordainment (ليلة القدر) from the highest heaven to the lowest heaven all at once, then it was divided over the years." In another wording: "Then it was sent down from the lowest heaven to the earth in stages (نجوما)." Then he recited: **فَلا أُقْسِمُ بِمَواقِعِ النُّجُومِ** ("I swear by the places where the stars set!").

**I say:** Its apparent meaning is the interpretation of "the places where the stars set" (مواقع النجوم) as the times of the descent of the stages of the Quran.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying: **فَلا أُقْسِمُ بِمَواقِعِ النُّجُومِ**. He said: "Its meaning is: I swear by the places where the stars set."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ibn Mardawayh narrated with a chain of transmission from Ibn Abbas from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): **إِنَّهُ لَقُرْآنٌ كَرِيمٌ فِي كِتابٍ مَكْنُونٍ**. He said: "**عند الله في صحف مطهرة** — 'With Allah in purified scrolls.'" **لا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ**. He said: "**المقربون** — 'Those brought near.'"

**I say:** The interpretation of "the pure ones" (المطهرون) as "those brought near" (المقربون) supports what we presented in the preceding commentary. We have already cited, under His saying: **هذا كِتابُنا يَنْطِقُ عَلَيْكُمْ بِالْحَقِّ** (45:29), a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) regarding the "protected Book."

In *al-Majma'*: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: **لا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ**. They said: "It is not permissible for the one in a state of major ritual impurity (جنب), the menstruating woman (حائض), or the one in a state of minor ritual impurity (محدث) to touch the *Mushaf* (the written Quran)." Narrated from Imam Muhammad ibn Ali (عليه‌السلام).

**I say:** What is meant by "touching the *Mushaf*" is touching its script, as evidenced by other narrations.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission from Dawud ibn Farqad from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام), he said: "I asked him about an amulet (التعويذ) hung upon a menstruating woman." He said: "**نعم لا بأس. وقال : تقرؤه وتكتبه ولا تصيبه يدها** — 'Yes, there is no harm.' And he said: 'She may read it and write it, but her hand should not touch it.'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Abd al-Razzaq, Ibn Abi Dawud, and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Abd Allah ibn Abi Bakr from his father, who said: "In the letter of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to Amr ibn Hazm: **ولا تمس القرآن إلا عن طهور** — 'And do not touch the Quran except in a state of purity.'"

**I say:** The narrations regarding this are numerous from both the Shi'i and Sunni paths.

In it, Muslim, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "It rained upon the people during the time of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), so the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: **أصبح من الناس شاكر ومنهم كافر** — 'This morning, some people are grateful and some are disbelievers.' They said: 'This is a mercy Allah has placed.' But some of them said: 'Such and such a star-phase (نوء) was truthful.' Then this verse was revealed: **فَلا أُقْسِمُ بِمَواقِعِ النُّجُومِ** until it reached **وَتَجْعَلُونَ رِزْقَكُمْ أَنَّكُمْ تُكَذِّبُونَ**."

**I say:** The narration has become widespread through Sunni paths that the verses were revealed regarding the star-phases (الأنواء). Its apparent meaning is that they are Medinan, but this does not harmonize with the context of the verses of the Surah, as you have seen.

In *al-Majma'*: The recitation of Ali (عليه‌السلام) and Ibn Abbas, and it is narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): **وتجعلون شكركم** ("and you make your gratitude").

**I say:** It is narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and Ali (عليه‌السلام).

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: Regarding His saying: **غَيْرَ مَدِينِينَ**. He said: "Its meaning is: if you were not to be recompensed for your deeds." **تَرْجِعُونَها** ("restore it")—meaning the spirit when it reaches the throat—"you return it to the body, **إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ** ('if you are truthful')."

In it, with its chain of transmission from Abu Basir, he said: "I heard Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) saying: **فَأَمَّا إِنْ كانَ مِنَ الْمُقَرَّبِينَ فَرَوْحٌ وَرَيْحانٌ** — 'in his grave,' **وَجَنَّةُ نَعِيمٍ** — 'in the Hereafter.'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: al-Qasim ibn Mandah narrated in *Kitab al-Ahwal wa al-Iman bi al-Su'al* from Salman, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'The first thing a believer is given glad tidings of at the time of death is ease, abundance, and a garden of bliss. And the first thing a believer is given glad tidings of in his grave is that it is said: "Receive glad tidings of the pleasure of Allah, the Exalted, and Paradise; you have come with the best of arrivals. Allah has forgiven those who escorted you to your grave, confirmed the one who bore witness for you, and answered the one who sought forgiveness for you."'"

In it, Ibn Jarir and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: **فَسَلامٌ لَكَ مِنْ أَصْحابِ الْيَمِينِ**. He said: "The angels come to him with peace from Allah—they greet him and inform him that he is among the companions of the right."

**I say:** The meaning he presented is based on the verse being a report of the speech of the angels. The implied meaning is: "The angels said: 'Peace be to you,' while you are among the companions of the right." Thus, it is both a greeting and glad tidings.
